_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Comprehensions practice 1
questions.explanation: The expression `n + 1` runs once for each input value. Adding one to 2, 4, and 6 produces 3, 5, and 7.

questions.title: Comprehensions practice 2
questions.prompt: Which expression creates a dictionary that maps each word to its length?
choices: {word: len(word) for word in words}
[word, len(word) for word in words]
{len(word) for word in words}
dict(word for len(word) in words)
questions.explanation: A dictionary comprehension needs a key expression, a colon, and a value expression before the for clause. Here each word is the key and its length is the value.

questions.title: Trace Comprehensions
questions.code:
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4]
squares = [number * number for number in numbers]
print(squares)
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: Read the comprehension as: make `number * number` for each number in the range. It creates a new list and does not change the range. The resulting output is '[1, 4, 9, 16]'.

questions.title: Comprehensions good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Comprehensions?
choices: Use a comprehension to create a new collection.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use a comprehension to create a new collection. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Comprehensions practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices:
even_squares = [n * n for n in numbers
                if n % 2 == 0]

result = [clean(x) if valid(x) else fix(x)
          for x in data if keep(x)]
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: The comprehension is useful because the job has one change and one filter. More steps would be easier to follow as a regular loop.

questions.title: Comprehensions practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Short does not always mean clear'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
A comprehension is a good fit when you can say its rule in one breath. Choose a normal loop when you need several conditions, temporary names, logging, or error handling.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: A comprehension is a good fit when you can say its rule in one breath. Choose a normal loop when you need several conditions, temporary names, logging, or error handling.

questions.title: Comprehensions practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Comprehensions?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Keep the expression and filter simple.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Keep the expression and filter simple. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Comprehensions practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Comprehensions?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Do not use one only to cause side effects.
questions.explanation: Do not use one only to cause side effects. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Comprehensions practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Comprehensions?
choices: Choose brackets for a list and braces for a set or dictionary.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Choose brackets for a list and braces for a set or dictionary. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Comprehensions practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Comprehensions?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Switch to a normal loop when the rule becomes hard to read.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Switch to a normal loop when the rule becomes hard to read. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
